data.title: Nutanix: Growth Beyond The Hype
data.description:
As Nutanix (NASDAQ:NTNX) trades around all-time highs, it becomes a question beyond value to be an investor now. The thesis should answer potential for growth from here on, compared to investments made post pandemic. The stock has had a relatively quiet year in 2024 compared to the Nasdaq 100. Whether that is a lull before the next leg up or further consolidation is what I will try to answer in this analysis.

Industry and Macro Environment

Nutanix's hybrid multicloud solution to simplify enterprise IT is a secular tailwind that could yet last in terms of total addressable market and growth opportunities. However, competition is also fierce (VMware Broadcom (AVGO), Dell Technologies (DELL), Microsoft (MST) and Cisco (CSCO), to name a few established names) and there's no true moat that cannot be penetrated. Differentiation exists in its offerings being platform and hardware agnostic to a large extent. However, there's no wide moat, particularly seen in comparison with larger players in the generic cloud space with proprietary cloud plays like AWS and Azure. Competition with players with deeper pockets and bigger ecosystems means Nutanix will have to grow organically over time and rely on customer stickiness and cross selling to grow.

The macro threat of reduced IT spending is less of a concern. Given Nutanix's offerings supporting what I believe to be the foundational or core IT layer in enterprises (computing, storage and networking), it should be able to withstand IT discretionary spending pauses. Hybrid solutions also reduce total cost of ownership while providing regulatory and reputational must-have security requirements against data sovereignty. As I see it, the biggest opportunity for Nutanix is among mid-sized enterprises, where the need for one-stop simplicity and flexibility is the highest. Smaller enterprises will mostly keep relying on on-premise infrastructure.

The opportunity for Nutanix opens up even in larger enterprises who were using VMWare and are facing issues around new license structures, price hikes, uncertain support and reliability going ahead. Nutanix's transparent pricing, flexibility (no lock-in) and lower complexity of offerings could see a huge interest as customers switch over from competition. However, given the transition hurdles and stickiness resultant thereof, the move over to Nutanix could take time and even see some drop offs if VMWare keeps allaying customer pain points in the upcoming months.

Key strategic partnerships with Cisco, Dell Technologies, and Pure Storage (PSTG) strengthen its platform capabilities and market reach, as highlighted in the recent earnings call.

Valuation with Digressing Stories

For valuation comparisons, I will still use erstwhile numbers of delisted VMWare and Hashicorp as they resemble the software only solutions business of Nutanix. Diverging for a bit while on VMWare and Hashicorp, two interesting angles are worth mentioning. Nutanix is almost a leading company now, focusing solely on hybrid cloud software solutions. While the clear focus helps build niche expertise and perception for Nutanix, VMWare and Hashicop (now with IBM (IBM)) will gain from cross selling opportunities among a larger client base of the new entities they're part of (maybe after initial hiccups). Plus the resource-rich advantages of a larger ecosystem.

The other interesting story is how Pure Storage is shaping up as both a partner as well as a competitor for Nutanix. With complementary offerings where they do not overlap, alliances to meet complex customer demand are not uncommon in the hybrid cloud business. It somewhat emphasized that the space is not a zero sum game where one large player takes over the market. There's room for decent growth for all players, prioritizing and solving for complementary customer needs. Partnering while still vying for the same wallets.

Coming back to valuations, NTNX currently trades at a significantly higher EV to forward revenue multiple of 7.9x compared to a peer like Pure Storage at 4.5x. This is understandable as Nutanix is seen as a subscription-based revenue generator relying on higher gross margins pure play in software. Pure Storage is transitioning into software and still has hardware legs to its business.

Nutanix also had a transformation into a more pure software play in its earlier days (from hardware offerings also as a focus point), which kept its valuation grounded in the past few years. It never saw the SaaS hype peaks that HashiCorp saw on its open source expectations. IBM's acquisition at lower valuations led to a reality check, and HashiCorp ended its listing close to Nutanix's valuations. Despite growth, as seen in HashiCorp, Nutanix was never valued above VMWare given the latter's deeper entrenched markets compared to Nutanix's early software foray days. All in all, Nutanix shows a history of no pumped up valuations. Recent highs relative to its own history are also not outrageously high and could be building in for higher growth short-term expectations of grabbing market share from VMWare post acquisition. Not the optimal valuations, but risk levels appear low in the overall context of the SaaS universe.

Financial Indicators Look Good

Q3 2025 results show a strong growth of 22% on its topline with 18% ARR growth. Revenue growth has been healthy for quite a few quarters now, even as NTNX scales. The recurring revenue from a subscription-based model is a big tick. Guidance for the last quarter of the FY is also positive, a revision upwards. The VMware effect of customers shifting into Nutanix is not hugely visible in top-line growth numbers. As pointed out earlier, it's a long lead process and may not translate fully as well. In fact, with time, VMWare and HashiCorp will potentially pressurize new market share for Nutanix given its larger ecosystem. The story is neither hugely threatening, nor materially bullish for NTNX, in a cloud space where dynamics are usually stable and changes don't happen one-sided toward a single provider or overnight.

Gross margins are looking extremely good, underlining the margin growth at scale story for Nutanix. Guidance on margins and cash flow also remains positive. Full year operating margins are expected at 20.5% with free cash flow of $715m at the midpoints.

NTNX Post Results Still a Hold

Despite a broad positive outlook, NTNX first corrected and then ended mostly flat in post market trade after results. This despite delivering strong results and raising full-year guidance. I believe NTNX shares have not broken out from current levels due to a mix of valuation concerns and investor expectations. The stock had already rallied nearly 29% year-to-date, prompting profit taking. I see no concerns around the valuations for the positives Nutanix offers.

Growth numbers look good, guidance is conservatively strong and the growth factors are supportive for future expansion as well. Cloud infrastructure solutions are relatively well shielded from any spending cut impact in an unhelpful macro scenario as well. It's more of a strategic necessity than a discretionary expense. So, while growth may decelerate marginally in the short term, the segment is unlikely to face significant long-term contraction, especially as businesses continue to prioritize their digital transformation efforts.

Overall, NTNX is a Buy. I'm comfortable with the stable dynamics of the cloud market as a support against the lack of any clear moat for NTNX. Valuations could have been better, but not a deal breaker. Buy with a long-term mindset on the secular tailwinds alone, if not the Nutanix story, with the ability to absorb short-term consolidation or corrections. The risk reward appears favorably aligned for fresh investments.

data.title: Plains All American Pipeline: A Great Option For Its Dividends
data.description:
Plains All American (NASDAQ:PAA) is an American company dedicated entirely to the midstream business of oil and gas transportation. The company maintains a presence with its pipelines in both Canada and the United States, although it concentrates the bulk of its business in the latter. As we will see later, the company's infrastructure is located primarily throughout the Permian Basin, from Alberta (Canada) to Texas (US).

As a pure-play MLP (midstream operator), Plains demonstrates a fairly stable cash flow thanks to the medium- to long-term contracts it maintains with oil and gas production companies. At the same time, this stability and predictability in the company's revenue is combined with a solid and well-developed infrastructure that primarily requires maintenance investments. All of this allows the company to reward shareholders with excellent dividend payments, as I will demonstrate throughout this report.

Thesis

As I mentioned earlier, Plains is a pure-play MLP, something I find truly attractive in a context of high volatility and sharp declines in international oil prices. Plains is one of the midstream companies with the highest market capitalization in the American market, with gas pipelines that crisscross North America from Alberta, Canada, to Texas in the United States. Analyzing the map below, you can see that the company maintains a presence throughout the Permian Basin, positioning it as a strategic infrastructure provider for the region's major oil and gas producers.

Now, from my perspective, midstream companies are attractive at a time when international fossil fuel prices are extremely volatile. While the drop in oil prices may have a short-term impact on the revenues of the basin's main producers, this scenario doesn't apply to companies like Plains. Infrastructure demand will continue, and there are a number of medium- and long-term contracts that offer a stability advantage in this context of increased price fluctuations.

In this sense, the question arises as to whether demand for fossil fuels will remain at current levels in the short and medium term or whether it will decline in the future. In the latter case, we could assume that the company's revenues could be affected in the short term. One thing we see is that the world's leading energy agencies maintain that demand for oil and gas will remain robust and even slightly increase in the future. As can be seen in the figure below, all of them, with the exception of the IEA, maintain that demand for fossil fuels will remain at current or higher levels until 2050.

Now, I think it's important to analyze the scenario from the supply side, because that's what will allow us to understand the situation of a company located in the middle of the production process, as is the case with Plains. When we look at the role of the United States as a producer, we see that it's one of the main producers. If we take into account that the Permian Basin is one of the main basins in the country, we understand the strategic role of this type of company. From a geographical perspective, the basin is located in the center of the country, which places it at a certain distance from export ports. In this context, fuel transportation infrastructure is essential.

Now, let's move on to what I consider to be one of the most important points when it comes to seeing Plains' potential compared to other companies in the fuel sector. Among the advantages I listed previously, the company's dividend yield and the shareholder payout policy it has demonstrated for several years undoubtedly stand out.

As I mentioned previously, the stable revenue generated by the company thanks to its business model has allowed management to pursue a shareholder payout policy that not only has a dividend yield that far exceeds the average for S&P 500 companies, but also grows.

As can be seen below, the gross dividend payout has increased consistently over the past few years. The amount is expected to continue to increase by 2025, based on the expected FCF the company can generate. This shareholder relationship is, in my view, one of the company's main advantages over other competitors. The other, as we will see later, is the multiples shown at the current share price.

1Q2025 Results

At this point, I'd like to briefly summarize the company's results for the last quarter. Plains reported net income of $443 million in 1Q2025, which represents a speculative increase compared to the $226 million reported in 1Q2024. However, looking at the Adjusted value, we see a 6% year-over-year increase, rising from a total of $354 million in 1Q2024 to $375 million in the latest report.

If we turn to Adjusted EBITDA, we see a similar trend. Year-over-year growth in this case was 4%, going from a total of $847 million in 1Q2024 to $881 million in 1Q2025. If we take this latter value and annualize it, we can compare it to the current EV value of $25.64 billion. In this case, taking this into account, we can see that the EV/EBITDA ratio is 7.27x. While this is somewhat higher compared to other companies I've covered, considering PAA's business model, it is interesting.

I wouldn't want to lose focus on the fact that Plains is a company with a business model that maintains a very stable revenue stream. In that sense, it's to be expected that the multiples are somewhat higher than those displayed by some cyclical companies more closely tied to fossil fuel production. Still, it's interesting to see that it's significantly below many S&P 500 companies, which, when combined with these fairly predictable revenues, is very promising.

Another highlight was net cash from operating activities, which closed the quarter at $639 million. Again, if we annualize this value, we see that the P/CF ratio is 4.62x, which, in my opinion, is attractive for a company with the PAA business model.

One of the things that could be concerning about the company is its debt stock relative to the company's Market Cap. However, as can be seen in the chart above, capital payments are well distributed over time in long-term debt, which, in my opinion, should not create pressure for the company. On the other hand, if we calculate the EBITDA/Interest ratio, we see that it stands at 6.21x, which, again, does not seem like a value that causes concern.

Valuation

At this point, I'd like to look at two models I created to give you an idea of ​​a possible valuation for the company and to gain a broader perspective on the investment thesis I detailed earlier. The first model I wanted to show is a DCF I created, taking into account the details in this report and some assumptions that aim to be as conservative as possible. For the other model, I created a valuation using multiples, using the market values ​​and ratios shown in the SeekingAlpha Peer segment as a reference.

I want to start with the DCF model, which, in my view, seems the most revealing of the company's potential. In that sense, the tax rate I used to perform the calculations was 15%, which, while seemingly low, is clearly above the average when compared to recent years in SeekingAlpha's Financials segment. Regarding the perpetual growth rate, I used a value of 2%, slightly below the average global GDP growth rate and significantly below the growth rate of the fossil fuel industry.

To discount cash flows, we must fully understand two things. First, the large proportion of debt within the capital structure, as previously seen. Second, regarding the cost of equity, it's important to emphasize again that the company is a service company with a fairly stable cash flow. For this reason, a WACC of 10% seems appropriate for the discount. This value, as can be seen, would be significantly higher than the cost of debt, which represents more than 90% of the company's EV. Therefore, I consider a 10% discount on cash flows to be more than conservative given the company's capital structure.

Regarding cash flows, I assumed EBIT growth of 4% in the first year, a growth that decreases over time until it converges to the perpetual growth rate. Regarding depreciation, a decrease over time was also assumed to provide a certain conservative effect to the model's results. Taking all of this into account, it is clear that the company has quite significant upside potential. If we take into account the rather predictable business model, we see that a valuation with these characteristics is more than attractive, in my view.

I didn't add values ​​to the Change in Operating Working Capital because the changes are very small compared to the other values, which are significant and representative for calculating FCF. Therefore, to simplify the model and estimates, I decided not to assign estimated values ​​for future periods and instead set them to zero.

Regarding the valuation multiples, I used companies listed in the SeekingAlpha Peers segment and the ratios presented there as a reference. The only thing worth mentioning is that for the final valuation, I weighted the ratios differently based on those I considered most relevant to explaining the company's metrics. Both the weights and the multiples are shown in the table below. What is interesting is that the upside potential is similar in both models, which in my view lends a certain degree of robustness to the assumptions used in the DCF.

Risks

Among the risks we could list is the sensitivity of the stock price to fluctuations in international oil prices. While it's true that this doesn't affect the company as directly as it might for producers, the markets have reacted negatively with a drop in the share price.

From my perspective, these movements add volatility but do not have a significant impact in the medium and long term, as the company's revenues are, as I mentioned previously, tied to longer-term contracts with production companies. On the other hand, the new tariff regulations that the Trump administration intends to push through have generated marked pessimism in the market.

Once again, these political ups and downs could negatively impact the price in the short term. However, it's important to note that PAA's core business is in the US, and I believe the impact of the new Republican administration's measures won't be as significant.

Conclusion

In general, it can be seen that Plains maintains a business structure that provides relatively stable cash flow in the short and medium term. This has allowed management to pursue an aggressive dividend payment policy that is beneficial to shareholders. Under this premise, Plains has maintained a high and upward dividend yield in recent years, something that is expected to continue to increase as long as FCF generation remains at current levels. Furthermore, with solid demand for fossil fuels and operating in the United States, in the Permian Basin, demand for Plains' infrastructure from the region's major producers is still expected to continue. For all these reasons, combined with a DCF model and a multiple valuation I conducted, I believe the company offers good upside potential for the future.

data.title: HFRO: Controversial Fund, But May Be Too Cheap With A Massive Discount To NAV
data.description:
HFRO Fund History

Highland Opportunities and Income Fund (NYSE:HFRO) is a controversial closed end fund that invests primarily in real estate assets but also holds a smattering of equity, CLOs, fixed and floating rate loans.

It has been about eighteen months since my last article on HFRO.

At the time, I rated HFRO a Hold and recommended a small starter position when it was at a 37% discount and adding more shares if the discount went above 40%. With the benefit of hindsight, it would have been much better to wait until later in 2024 after a surprise 50% distribution cut in January 2024 caused a mass exit from dividend oriented investors.

Overall, the HFRO NAV and market performance has been pretty dismal since late 2023, but there have been some trading opportunities since then to help alleviate the damage. There was a tender offer earlier this year, where you could have exchanged about 46% of your shares for preferred stock.

I decided to write an update now, because I believe the HFRO management may soon be initiating a new share repurchase program. With a 57% discount to NAV, such a program appears to be a no-brainer.

For quite some time now, HFRO has been trading with a discount well above 50% which is the highest discount in the US closed-end fund universe tracked on cefconnect.com.

Why is the Discount So High?

  • Very High Exposure To Illiquid Assets: In the last holdings reports to shareholders as of March 31, 2025. HFRO included the following blurb in a small footnote:

    • “Securities with a total aggregate value of $757,415,160, or 87.4% of net assets, were classified as Level 3 within the three-tier fair value hierarchy”.

    • Note that a significant portion of the Level 3 assets are secured by real estate which is more difficult to value accurately than publicly traded stocks or bonds.

    • Many investors are especially concerned in this case because several of the Level 3 illiquid assets are controlled or related to the NexPoint and Highland management companies. Management benefits from higher management fees if the NAV fair value marks to market are too high.

  • Distribution Cut: The discount widened a lot after a large 50% distribution cut at the end of 2023, and has never really recovered. A higher distribution returns more capital to shareholders allowing them to recover more of the discount. The distribution cut rubbed a lot of income investors the wrong way and they likely dumped their HFRO shares.

  • Highly Complex Portfolio: HFRO holds a widely diverse set of assets which may be hard to understand. Management does do a pretty good job in a quarterly shareholder report of describing the top ten holdings in some detail, but the diversity of assets in both real estate and non-real estate can be difficult to value.

  • Shareholder Concerns About Persistent Discount: The fund management has taken some limited actions to reduce the discount, but has not been aggressive enough in returning capital to shareholders.

    • Two years ago, management initiated a $100 million share buyback program. But only about $20 million of this program was actually utilized, and the buyback program has just ended. I recently sent a message to Shareholder Relations asking if they will be extending the share buyback program going forward.

    • The fund instituted a $100 million tender offer for shareholders this year offering $10 a share. But the $10 a share was not paid in cash and no portfolio assets had to be liquidated. Instead they issued $100 million of a new 5.375% preferred stock (HFRO.PR.B). By doing this, the fund management did not have to return any cash to shareholders, so the management fee which is based on assets under management remained the same. When the new preferred came out, the market price traded around $16, so the effective price that investors could have realized was about $6.40 a share, not $10.00.

  • The “Dondero” Discount: Two closed-end funds run by Highland Capital Management, (NYSE:HFRO) and (HGLB) tend to have high persistent discounts to NAV. There has been a lot of litigation involved in companies controlled by James Dondero. For example Highland Capital Management, an investment advisor controlled by Dondero, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in October, 2019. While this did not directly hurt shareholders in HFRO or HGLB, such events can erode investor confidence.

The recent market performance of HFRO has been mixed, partially because swings in the discount have a much larger effect than the actual NAV performance. More active closed-end fund traders may have benefited from the recent $10 tender offer with a proration of about 46%. It would have been possible to sell off their preferred shares above $16.00 (equivalent to a common share price of $6.40) the first few weeks after the tender offer closed.

Some Recent Developments

1) Since the distribution cut at the end of 2023, the fund has paid out regular monthly distributions of $0.0385. Management has not discussed raising the distribution again. I suspect they would prefer share buybacks which gives them more flexibility in how much is returned to shareholders.

2) The Credit Suisse case, which seems to drag on forever, is back in the Texas fifth circuit court of appeals. The Fund has provided a link for any shareholder who wants to follow the Credit Suisse litigation in detail- TJB | 5th COA The court number is 05-24-01124-CV.

If there are any attorneys reading this article, perhaps they can comment on this case. As a non-attorney, I felt that it is quite possible that HFRO may not receive anything additional, or perhaps only a small amount. Perhaps a compromise will be reached to reduce the legal fees for both sides.

3) The two year $100 million share buyback expired in May 2025. I have sent a note to Investor Relations asking/suggesting that a new share repurchase agreement should be initiated since the discount to NAV is well above 50%.

Some investors would likely feel this is a waste of my time. But I was surprised back in 2023. Here is an excerpt from an old HFRO article I wrote back in November, 2023:

“There were no share repurchases in the second quarter of 2023. I contacted Investor Relations and asked them if they would consider providing more frequent updates of their share repurchase activities. This is done by several other closed-end funds. For example, SPE provides monthly updates of their share buyback activity.

They told me that for now, they only plan to provide updates in the annual and semi-annual shareholder reports. Perhaps if other shareholders request more frequent share buyback updates, the Fund's Board will reconsider this policy.”

I was pleasantly surprised that HFRO did start to report the share repurchase activity in 2024 on a quarterly basis. I would have preferred monthly reporting, similar to SPE, but the quarterly updates are a big improvement over what we had before.

Three Year Discount History for HFRO

Here is the asset allocation breakdown as of March 31, 2025:

Portfolio Allocation Breakdown

Number of Holdings= 72

Leverage Percentage= 26.4%

Source: HFRO Fund Fact Sheet Q1 2025

Real estate is the largest allocation category by far. A more detailed summary of the real estate holdings was presented in the last shareholder presentation. The largest allocations are to Multifamily, Self Storage, Single family Rentals and Life Sciences with little or no allocation to the troubled retail and commercial office sectors.

Portfolio - Top 10 Exposures (as of March 31, 2025)

I know that many investors are highly suspect of the NAV reported by HFRO. But I believe that the NAV reported by HFRO management is probably pretty close to being accurate. A precise valuation of any private investment is always difficult and adds a lot of uncertainty to the NAV.

Keep in mind that the NAV uncertainty can occur in both directions, so it is even possible that The NAV could be understated depending on how things play out in the future with the economy.

The current 57% discount seems more than adequate to account for the added NAV uncertainty, especially if management re-enacts the share repurchase program. Of course this is only true if management actually starts to aggressively buy back shares in the open market.

Trading Opportunities in HFRO

I have owned a core position in HFRO for some time now. Because of big swings in its discount to NAV, there are sometimes opportunities to add shares on a sharp downward move in the price, or to trim shares when the stock bounces up rapidly.

The $100 million tender offer earlier this year also provided some good opportunities, especially for those who aggressively sold the HFRO-B preferred when it first started to trade. I did trim a few of the HFRO-B shares from the tender offer north of $16, but in hindsight I was not nearly aggressive enough. With the preferred trading closer to $15, I am holding my remaining shares and would look to add more shares on dips below $15.

Fund Performance

Here is the year-by-year total return performance record since 2018 when the fund converted to the closed-end fund format. Performance has been poor on both an absolute and relative basis in 2023 and 2024, after a good year in 2022.

Morningstar uses the Bank Loan category for HFRO, but they should probably move HFRO to one of the real estate categories, since the fund has changed considerably over the past few years. The performance numbers do not take into account any gains an investor may have made from the tender offer in 2024.

Alpha is Generated by High Discount + High Distributions

When you invest in HFRO, there is a possibility of capturing some "alpha" from the high discount and distribution yield.

The distribution rate of 9.13% along with the 57.6% discount allows investors to capture some alpha by recovering a small portion of the NAV whenever a monthly distribution is paid out.

When you recover NAV from a fund selling at a 57.6% discount, the percentage gain is 1.00/ 0.424 or about 135.8%. So the annual alpha generated by the 9.13% distribution is computed as:

(0.0913)*(1.358)=0.1239 or about 12.39% a year in discount alpha.

This is way more than the 1.45% baseline expense ratio. As long as the HFRO managers can stabilize performance, the fund should be able to generate an attractive total return going forward.

Future Potential For Activist Activity

Institutions hold about 51.7% of the shares, which is up considerably over the last few years.

Here are some opportunistic investors that owned shares in HFRO as of March 31, 2025:

Activist # of shares
